does: This paper evaluates how vulnerable current large language models are to generating personalized disinformation by systematically testing both open-source and closed-source LLMs on their ability to create fake news articles tailored to specific audiences. The authors assess whether LLMs can reliably generate personalized disinformation content, whether models can self-evaluate the quality of their personalization, and whether adding personalization elements makes generated disinformation harder to detect as synthetic. The study reveals significant vulnerabilities across tested models, demonstrating that safety filters fail to adequately prevent this combination of capabilities and that disclaimers alone are insufficient defenses.
connects: This work extends prior research showing that LLMs can generate convincing disinformation and that they excel at producing personalized content across various dimensions. Previous studies established baseline concerns about LLM misuse for fake news generation and documented personalization abilities, but stopped short of examining the intersection of these two risks. The paper builds on safety evaluation frameworks used to probe model vulnerabilities and integrates insights from work on content personalization, synthetic text detection, and meta-evaluation capabilities. It responds directly to the gap where personalization and disinformation capabilities had been studied separately, providing the first comprehensive assessment of how their combination creates novel risks.
enables: This evaluation points toward developing more robust safety mechanisms specifically designed to detect and prevent personalized harm generation, moving beyond generic content filters. The findings motivate research into detection methods that account for personalization tactics and into red-teaming methodologies that test adversarial combinations of model capabilities rather than isolated ones. The work establishes benchmarks for measuring LLM vulnerabilities to multi-dimensional misuse that future models must address, supporting development of stronger alignment techniques. It also enables policy discussions about appropriate guardrails for personalization features and informs the design of safer deployment practices that incorporate adversarial awareness of how different capabilities can compound risks.
